mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Bolivia on?
mapQuestions.answer: South America, in the centre. Bolivia is landlocked (no coastline), high up in the Andes, with Brazil, Argentina, Chile, Peru and Paraguay around it 🌎

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Bolivia?
mapQuestions.answer: Bolivia has TWO capitals! Sucre is the constitutional capital, but the government actually sits in La Paz, the world's highest capital city at 3,640 metres above sea level.

mapQuestions.question: What languages does Bolivia speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Spanish + 36 indigenous languages, all official! The biggest are Quechua and Aymara, both still widely spoken in the highlands. Bolivia has more indigenous-language speakers (proportionally) than any other South American country.

mapQuestions.question: What is the Salar de Uyuni?
mapQuestions.answer: The biggest salt flat in the world: 10,000 square km of pure white salt up at 3,650 m altitude. When it rains, it turns into the world's biggest mirror, reflecting the sky perfectly.

mapQuestions.question: What is Lake Titicaca?
mapQuestions.answer: The highest navigable lake in the world (3,812 m), shared with Peru. The Aymara people built floating reed islands on it, and have lived on Lake Titicaca for thousands of years.

foods.name: Salteña
foods.description: A Bolivian baked pastry like a slightly sweet, slightly soupy empanada, filled with stewed meat, potato, peas, hard-boiled egg, olive and a touch of broth. Tricky to eat without spilling. Eaten mid-morning, never for dinner.

foods.name: Pique macho
foods.description: "Macho bite": a giant shared plate of beef chunks, sausages, boiled egg, tomato, onion, chilli, mustard and tomato sauce, all piled on top of fried potatoes. Cochabamba's signature dish.

foods.name: Trucha del Titicaca
foods.description: Lake Titicaca trout, grilled or pan-fried, served with quinoa and potatoes. The high-altitude lake produces firm pink trout you find nowhere else.

foods.name: Chuño
foods.description: Freeze-dried highland potatoes! Aymara and Quechua peoples have been freeze-drying potatoes in the cold dry mountain air for over 2,000 years to make chuño, then reconstituting it in stews. The world's oldest potato preservation technique.

foods.name: Llajwa
foods.description: A spicy fresh tomato-and-chilli salsa that Bolivians put on everything. Made fresh daily in a stone mortar called a "batán".

foods.name: Api
foods.description: A hot thick purple drink made from purple maize, sweetened with cinnamon and cloves. Sold from street stalls in the morning with fried dough fritters called "pasteles".

scavengerItems.label: The blinding-white Salar de Uyuni salt flat stretching to the horizon
A llama or alpaca on the altiplano (high plateau)
A "cholita" woman in bowler hat, layered skirts and bright woven shawl
Snow-capped Andean peaks (6,000+ m mountains all around La Paz)
The Mi Teleférico cable cars of La Paz (the world's longest urban cable-car system)
A street parade with traditional dance and brass band
A giant cardón cactus on a Salar island
The mirror-like salt flat after rain reflecting the sky
A snow-capped volcano on the Chilean border
The yungas valleys (the cloud forest road down from the Andes to the Amazon)
Pink flamingos at the Eduardo Avaroa high-altitude lakes
A reed boat ("balsa de totora") on Lake Titicaca

geoMatches.description: The de facto capital, 3,640 m up in a deep canyon. Houses run up the walls of the bowl as far as the eye can see. The world's highest big city.
The largest salt flat on Earth, 10,000 square km of pure white salt at 3,650 m altitude. Mirror in the rainy season, blinding white desert in the dry.
The world's highest navigable lake, shared with Peru. The Aymara "Isla del Sol" island is said to be where the Inca sun god was born.
The constitutional capital, in the south. A beautiful Spanish colonial city of white-washed buildings (the "white city"), narrow streets and red roofs.
A historic silver-mining city at 4,067 m altitude. For 300 years its mountain ("Cerro Rico") produced silver that funded the Spanish Empire. UNESCO World Heritage.
The steep valleys on the eastern side of the Andes where the mountains drop into the Amazon. Coca leaf farms, coffee, and the famous "Death Road" downhill biking route.

senses.smell: Coca leaves being brewed into mate de coca, woodsmoke at dusk in a highland village, dry mountain air at altitude, roast llama on a market stall, wet earth in the Yungas after rain…
senses.hear: Pan flutes echoing up Andean valleys, Aymara and Quechua mixed with Spanish, the soft chuff of a salt-flat 4x4, "¡Cholita!" "¡Caserito!" in markets, brass bands in street parades…
senses.taste: Hot purple api drink, smoky pique macho, sharp llajwa salsa, freeze-dried chuño potato in soup, sweet coconut-flavoured singani-soda (a non-alcoholic local treat)…
senses.feel: Thin Andean air that makes you breathless your first day, hot sun and ice-cold shade at altitude, the rough white crystals of the Salar de Uyuni, the soft alpaca wool of an aguayo blanket…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: How is the world's biggest mirror also a salt desert?
stories.body: About 40,000 years ago, this part of the Andes was a giant lake (Lake Minchin). As the climate slowly dried out, the lake evaporated, leaving behind 10 BILLION tonnes of salt spread over 10,000 square kilometres at 3,650 m altitude. That is the Salar de Uyuni. In the dry season (May to November) it is a blinding white salt desert, with hexagonal patterns where the salt crust has cracked and re-formed. In the rainy season (December to April) a thin film of water sits on top of the salt, and the whole thing turns into the world's biggest natural mirror, perfectly reflecting the sky, the clouds and your shoes. Below the salt crust is one of the largest lithium reserves on Earth, which is making the Salar increasingly important to the world's battery industry.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Beneath the salt is enough lithium to power millions of batteries. Should Bolivia dig it up, or leave it as a mirror?

stories.question: How do you live in the world's highest capital?
stories.body: La Paz sits in a deep bowl in the altiplano (high plateau), with the rich neighbourhoods at the BOTTOM of the bowl (where the air is thicker and warmer) and the poorer neighbourhoods at the top, up to 4,000 m. The flat city of El Alto, on the rim, is one of the highest cities of its size on Earth. Locals climb between levels by walking, by taxi, or now by the famous "Mi Teleférico" cable car system, which runs nearly 30 km across the city in different colour-coded lines. Newcomers feel altitude sickness for days, dizzy, headache, sleepless. Locals laugh, drink coca-leaf tea, and carry on. Watching the sun set over La Paz from a cable car is unforgettable.

stories.question: Who are the "cholitas"?
stories.body: A "cholita" is an indigenous Aymara or Quechua woman in traditional dress: a stiff bowler hat (perched cleverly on the head, never tied), several layered skirts called "polleras", a long braided plait, a bright woven shawl ("aguayo") used as a baby carrier, market basket, or coat. The bowler hat tradition started by accident in the 1920s, when a shipment of European bowler hats arrived in Bolivia too small for European men, and were given to indigenous women instead. They adopted them and never looked back. Today cholitas are everywhere in Bolivia, running market stalls, riding taxis, becoming politicians and even wrestling each other in famous cholita-wrestling shows in El Alto.

stories.question: How do people live on floating reed islands?
stories.body: The Uros people have lived on Lake Titicaca for over 2,000 years, on islands they build themselves out of totora reeds that grow in the shallow water at the lake's edge. Each island is layers and layers of dried reeds, anchored to the bottom of the lake, where bundles rot from below and new ones are added on top constantly (about every two weeks). The Uros build their houses, boats, even their cooking stoves out of reeds. They originally moved out onto the lake to escape the warlike Inca, and have lived there ever since. About 40 islands are still inhabited, and you can visit them by boat from Puno (Peru) or Copacabana (Bolivia).

stories.question: Why did one mountain shape world history?
stories.body: In 1545, Spanish colonists discovered silver in Cerro Rico ("Rich Mountain"), a 4,800-metre peak in southern Bolivia. Over the next 300 years, the mountain produced more silver than anywhere else on Earth: an estimated 60,000 tonnes. The city of Potosí below it became, briefly, one of the biggest and richest cities in the world (bigger than London or Paris). The silver was shipped to Spain (where it funded wars and palaces), and across the Pacific to China (where it became the basis for the global silver-trade economy). Cerro Rico is still mined today, but it is mostly empty after centuries of digging. Hundreds of thousands of indigenous people and enslaved Africans died in those mines. The mountain is now a UNESCO World Heritage site, and a haunting reminder of how one rock changed the world.

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Bolivia so far?
Have you tried saying "gracias" or "sulpayki" yet?
Salar, La Paz, Titicaca, Sucre, which has felt the most "Bolivia"?
How does it feel walking around at 3,500+ m altitude?
Cholita hats, bright skirts, pan flutes, llamas, which is the most "Bolivia" image for you?
Potosí silver paid for Spanish palaces 5,000 km away. How can one rock have such a big effect?

wordSearch.hint: Bolivian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Llama
animals.description: Across the altiplano, used for carrying loads and for wool. Sometimes wearing colourful ear tassels.

animals.name: Alpaca
animals.description: Fluffier than a llama, with much softer fleece. In the highest highland pastures.

animals.name: Andean condor
animals.description: One of the world's biggest flying birds (3 m wingspan), soaring above the Andean peaks.

animals.name: Andean / James's / Chilean flamingo
animals.description: Bolivia is one of the few places where all THREE South American flamingo species live, at the high salt lakes near Uyuni.

animals.name: Vicuña
animals.description: The wild ancestor of the alpaca, with the finest wool in the world. Protected on the altiplano.

animals.name: Pink river dolphin
animals.description: In the Bolivian Amazon (the Pampas) around Rurrenabaque.

animals.name: Jaguar
animals.description: In the Madidi National Park rainforests, although extremely hard to spot.
